text: Being placed in a random group with strangers feels comfortable to you.
During breaks between classes, you prefer finding a quiet spot over joining classmates.
You are usually the one who suggests plans like lunch or group study sessions.
When a classmate you barely know starts a conversation, you engage willingly.
After a day of group work and socialising, you need several hours alone to recover.
You have introduced yourself to someone at university purely out of curiosity.
When your friend group plans a large gathering, you secretly hope something comes up so you can skip it.
You genuinely enjoy learning about the backgrounds and opinions of people you meet at university.
If you disagreed with something a lecturer said, you would speak up in front of the class.
You mentally rehearse what to say before approaching someone you do not know well.
After a group presentation, the interaction with the audience felt more rewarding than the finished work.
When walking across campus alone, you put headphones in to signal you are unavailable.
You have naturally taken on an organiser role in a study group without being asked.
When stressed about an exam, your first instinct is to call or meet a friend.
Being around people for long periods gradually wears you down, even people you like.
At a university event where you know few people, you approach strangers rather than wait.
You enjoy moments in class when discussion gets lively and people are openly debating.
You feel quietly relieved when a planned social event gets cancelled.
Your friends would describe you as someone who brings energy to a group setting.
Your ideal weekend during semester involves spending most of the time with other people.
